records.notes: Cold-weather gilled mushroom on wood.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Wild velvet foot is edible when confidently identified and cooked, but it is a winter gilled mushroom with deadly look-alikes. Keep wild brown-stemmed forms separate from cultivated enoki in your head.
records.edibility_detail: Confirm clustered growth on wood, orange-brown sticky caps, white spore print, no ring, and a dark velvety stem base. Do not eat any Galerina-like brown gilled mushroom.
records.general_notes: The dark velvety stem base matters here. This is one of the few edible-type gilled mushrooms worth checking in cold weather.

mushroom_profile.season_note: October, November, December, January, February, March.

records.field_identification: Small to medium orange-brown caps in clusters on dead hardwood, often in cold weather. Gills are pale, spore print white, there is no ring, and the lower stem becomes dark and velvety.
records.mushroom_card_note: Winter edible only with careful ID; deadly Galerina is the ugly little goblin in this room.
records.caution_reason: Deadly Galerina look-alike risk; spore print must be white.
records.look_alike_notes: Do not eat wild enoki without confirming a white spore print and a dark velvety ringless stem; deadly galerina grows on wood too and has rusty-brown spores.
records.quality_notes: Good winter edible when correctly confirmed; cultivated enoki looks very different from wild velvet foot.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook; spore print and full winter ID checks are required.
records.risk_reason: Wild enoki grows on wood in cold weather and can be confused with deadly Galerina and other little brown mushrooms.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If confused with Galerina: delayed amatoxin poisoning affecting liver and kidneys, with possible liver failure and death.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Brownish/orange sticky cap, wood-growing habit, late fall/winter season, and overlap with small brown wood-growing mushrooms. Wild enoki should have a white spore print and a dark velvety stem that darkens from the base upward.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat wild enoki without a confirmed white spore print and velvet-foot stem traits. Any brown/rust spore print means do not eat.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — deadly look-alike possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Wild velvet foot/enoki overlaps with Deadly Galerina in wood habitat, so spore print and stem traits are mandatory.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Reject any rusty-brown spore print, ring zone, greenish gills, non-velvety stem base, or warm-season wood cluster that does not fit velvet foot.

lookalike_traits: Ring or ring zone may be present
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Any rusty-brown spore print means do not eat.
Greenish gills or purple-brown spores mean do not eat.

records.culinary_uses: Choice edible chanterelle-type mushrooms when positively identified. Cook; do not confuse with jack-o'-lanterns or true-gilled orange mushrooms.
records.field_identification: Ground-growing chanterelle relatives with blunt false gills, ridges, wrinkles, or a smooth underside rather than thin blade-like true gills. The false gills usually run down the stem, fork, cross-vein, or look melted into the cap. Flesh is usually firm and pale inside, often with a fruity or apricot-like smell. Do not identify by orange/yellow color alone.
records.mushroom_card_note: Conifer-associated yellow chanterelle complex; confirm blunt false gills, not true blade-like gills.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer through fall.
taste: mild to slightly peppery in some species; not a primary beginner ID trait

records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when confidently identified by false gills/ridges, ground growth, firm flesh, and appropriate habitat. Do not eat orange mushrooms with true blade-like gills or mushrooms growing in dense clusters from wood or buried roots.

records.look_alike_notes: Main concern is jack-o’-lantern mushrooms. Jack-o’-lanterns have true blade-like gills, often grow in dense clusters from wood, stumps, buried roots, or tree bases, and are typically deeper pumpkin-orange. Chanterelles grow from the ground and have blunt ridges or false gills rather than separable true gills. False chanterelles and other orange gilled mushrooms should also be separated by true gills, softer texture, and different growth habits.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject slimy, brown-edged, waterlogged, moldy, or bug-heavy specimens. Reject specimens where the underside has sharp, separable true gills. Reject orange clustered mushrooms from wood, stumps, or buried roots.
records.image_needs: Needs underside macro showing false gills/ridges, side view showing ridges running down stem, split specimen showing pale/solid flesh, habitat shot showing ground growth, and chanterelle vs jack-o’-lantern comparison.
records.quality_notes: High-value table mushrooms when firm, fresh, and not waterlogged.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook fresh; useful sautéed, dried, or preserved depending on species.
records.risk_reason: The main danger is confusing true chanterelles with jack-o’-lanterns or false chanterelles. Jack-o’-lanterns are poisonous.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If confused with jack-o’-lantern: gastrointestinal tract, with cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: True chanterelles have blunt, forked ridges or false gills that look melted into the underside, grow from soil, and are usually singly or loosely scattered. Jack-o’-lanterns grow in dense clusters from wood or buried roots and have sharp true gills.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat any “chanterelle” unless the underside has blunt ridges, not blade-like gills, and the mushroom is not growing from wood.
records.caution_reason: The main danger is confusing true chanterelles with jack-o’-lanterns or false chanterelles. Jack-o’-lanterns are poisonous.

lookalike_risk.label: High — poisonous orange-gilled look-alike possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Chanterelles must be separated from jack-o’-lanterns and false chanterelles by underside, flesh, and substrate.
required_checks: Confirm blunt forked ridges, wrinkles, folds, or nearly smooth underside
Confirm ground growth, not dense wood clusters
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: For yellow/smooth chanterelles, sharp true gills or dense orange wood clusters mean stop.

target_traits: Blunt forked ridges, wrinkles, folds, or nearly smooth underside
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Wood clusters with sharp true orange gills are not chanterelles.
Sharp separable true gills mean do not eat as chanterelle.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Choice spring edible when confirmed as a true morel. Clean well, slice lengthwise, and cook thoroughly; do not eat raw or undercooked.
records.general_notes: Representative Commons images currently use Morchella esculenta / yellow-morel-type material for this field-guide group record.
records.look_alike_notes: Every morel must be cut lengthwise before eating; discard any specimen that is not hollow from cap tip to stem base or has a wrinkled/brain-like cap instead of true pits. Link false morels/Gyromitra and Verpa-style early false morels as primary dangers.
records.quality_notes: Excellent spring mushroom when fresh and firm; quality drops when old, dry, buggy, sandy, or mushy.
records.eating_use_notes: Clean well, cut lengthwise, inspect each specimen, and cook thoroughly. Try a small first serving.
records.risk_reason: False morels and Verpa-type look-alikes can be poisonous. Even true morels can cause gastrointestinal upset if raw, undercooked, eaten in large amounts, or possibly combined with alcohol.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If confused with false morels: gastrointestinal tract, liver, kidneys, nervous system, and blood. If true morels are raw/undercooked: gastrointestinal upset.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Morel-like mushrooms may be wrinkled, lobed, chambered, or attached differently. True morels should be hollow from cap tip through stem when cut lengthwise, with the cap properly attached to the stem.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat morels raw or undercooked. Do not eat any “morel” unless every specimen is cut lengthwise and confirmed as true Morchella.
records.caution_reason: False morels and Verpa-type look-alikes can be poisonous. Even true morels can cause gastrointestinal upset if raw, undercooked, eaten in large amounts, or possibly combined with alcohol.

edible_use.notes: Choice spring edible after confident true-morel identification. Slice every specimen lengthwise to confirm a hollow interior and proper cap attachment; cook thoroughly and do not eat raw.

records.redirect_note: Yellow morels are covered under the canonical white-yellow-morels record.

records.edibility_detail: Edible when confidently identified and cooked, but this entry is a group-level field record because North American wood-ear names have shifted. Host wood and overall jelly-ear form matter more here than old European names in field guides.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when confidently identified and cooked; valued more for chewy texture than flavor. Use fresh, clean specimens from sound wood, or dry and rehydrate them for soups, stir-fries, and brothy dishes. Avoid old, blackened, polluted, or slimy-decomposing specimens.
records.overview: Wood ears are brown, rubbery-gelatinous fungi that form ear-shaped, cup-shaped, or irregular floppy fruiting bodies on dead wood after wet weather. In North America, older field guides often used European wood-ear names, but current sources separate similar-looking hardwood and conifer species. For field use in Upper Michigan, this entry treats them as a wood-ear group and leans on substrate: hardwood logs and branches suggest an Auricularia angiospermarum-type wood ear, while conifer wood suggests an Auricularia americana-type wood ear.
records.notes: Brown ear-shaped to cup-shaped jelly fungi on dead wood; substrate matters because North American wood-ear taxonomy separates hardwood and conifer associates more than older field guides did.
records.general_notes: A field-useful wood-ear group entry. Record the host wood when possible: hardwood and conifer collections may represent different North American Auricularia species even when they look nearly identical.
records.field_identification: Look for thin brown to reddish-brown gelatinous ears, cups, or floppy lobes attached directly to dead wood. The outer surface may look finely hairy or frosted when fresh; the fertile surface is smoother and often wrinkled or veined. They dry dark and tough, then revive after rain. Note the host wood carefully, because substrate is one of the best field clues for separating North American wood-ear species.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring through fall after wet weather; dried fruiting bodies can persist and revive after rain.

use_links.notes: Hardwood wood-ear page and comparison with conifer-associated A. americana.

records.look_alike_notes: Brown jelly fungi such as Exidia species can look similar, but they usually form more blob-like, brain-like, or cushiony masses rather than thin ear-shaped cups. Leafy Tremella-like jelly fungi can form clustered folds, but they are usually more frilly or fused into masses instead of distinct ear-like lobes. Substrate and shape matter more here than color alone.

lookalike_traits: Brittle or leathery cup on soil, not gelatinous wood ear

records.scientific_name: Lactarius / Lactifluus spp.; includes volemus-type, hygrophoroides-type, indigo, orange-latex, and related milkcap groups
records.edibility_detail: Some milk caps are good edibles, but this group stays caution-level unless the entry is a well-supported species. Confirm latex color, latex color-change, gill spacing, odor, host tree, and staining behavior. Avoid acrid/hot, poorly identified, old, dry, or latex-free specimens unless the species is confirmed by other traits.
records.culinary_uses: Some milk caps are good edibles when confidently identified and cooked, but the group is not beginner-simple. Avoid acrid, peppery, bitter, or poorly identified milk caps.
records.overview: Broad edible/caution group for milk caps because Lactarius/Lactifluus are a major missing gilled category and require latex-color filtering.
records.notes: Practical field treatment is safer than pretending every orange-brown, mild milkcap can be cleanly species-keyed in the app. Lactifluus volemus is recognizable when classic, but similar edible/nearby taxa differ in gill spacing, odor, and latex staining.
records.general_notes: Broad edible/caution group for milk caps because Lactarius/Lactifluus are a major missing gilled category and require latex-color filtering.
records.field_identification: Gilled mushrooms that exude latex or “milk” when the gills or flesh are cut. Identification depends heavily on latex color, how quickly the latex changes color, whether it stains the flesh or paper, cap zoning, gill spacing, odor, taste/acridity, and host trees. Do not identify milk caps by cap color alone.

gill_color: Pale cream to yellowish or orange-tinged; attached to decurrent; close in L. volemus, more distant in L. hygrophoroides.
stem_color: Colored like cap or paler; solid to stuffed; no ring or volva.
latex_color_initial: scant or absent in old/dry specimens
latex_color_change: orange with no green staining in Lactarius thyinos
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Latex can be scant in old or dry specimens, so absence of milk is not a confident ID.
staining: Classic L. volemus develops brown staining on cut tissue, hands, paper, and damaged areas. Some similar taxa remain paler or stain less strongly.
host_filter_tokens: hardwoods, especially oaks for L. hygrophoroides
mixed eastern North American hardwood woods
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall.
stem_features: same color as cap or paler

records.look_alike_notes: Milk caps are a field-ID system, not a single trait. Latex color and color-change timing are critical. Some species have white latex that turns yellow or brown; some orange-latex species stain green; Lactarius thyinos is notable for orange milk without green bruising. Acrid or hot-tasting milk caps should not be treated as edible beginner species. The true European Lactarius deliciosus name has often been misapplied in North America, so use regional North American names/groups when possible.

edible_use.notes: Edible group for advanced confirmation only; use this entry only when it shows mild taste, white latex behavior, no acrid burn, no yellowing latex, and no ring/volva. Not beginner-safe by color alone.

records.image_needs: Needs fresh cut gills showing latex, same cut after 30 seconds/5 minutes/30–60 minutes, latex staining paper/tissue, cap zoning/scrobicules, gill spacing comparison, and host/habitat images.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Old/dry specimens may not produce latex; do not force an ID from dried-out material. Reject moldy, waterlogged, sour-smelling, insect-heavy, or weakly identified specimens. Avoid acrid/hot milk caps unless the app has a specific, regionally appropriate edible treatment.
records.quality_notes: Quality varies by species; some are good, some grainy, acrid, bitter, or poor.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook confirmed edible milkcap groups. Do not use generic latex as permission to eat.
records.risk_reason: Milkcaps are a large, difficult group. Latex color, latex staining, taste, odor, host tree, and timing all matter.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Some toxic or irritating milkcaps can cause gastrointestinal and autonomic symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, salivation, sweating, vertigo, or cold feeling.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Latex may be white, colored, watery, scant, or absent in old/dry specimens. Staining reactions can take up to an hour. Acrid, bitter, strongly peppery, or uncertain milkcaps should not be eaten.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat milk caps based only on “it has latex.” Only eat species/groups the app treats as confirmed edible, and reject acrid, bitter, strongly peppery, or unidentified milk caps.
records.caution_reason: Group complexity and latex/staining/taste/host-tree dependence; caution is ID risk, not food value.

records.overview: Bulbous honey mushroom is an Armillaria species usually found on the ground or near hardwood bases, often attached to buried roots. It tends to be smaller and looser than classic honey mushroom clusters, with a swollen/bulbous stem base and a cobwebby or short-lived ring zone.

records.general_notes: Species-level split for the honey mushroom complex so the app does not treat all honeys as one vague bucket.

records.look_alike_notes: Honey mushrooms have white spores and Armillaria-style growth on wood, roots, or buried wood. Deadly galerina has rusty-brown spores and small brown caps; sulfur tuft has yellow-green gills and purple-brown spores; brick cap has gray to purple-gray gills and brick-red caps; shaggy scalycap has heavy scales and cinnamon-brown spores; jack-o-lanterns are orange, clustered on wood, and poisonous. Do not eat honey mushrooms unless a mature cap gives a white spore print; deadly galerina can grow on the same wood and has a rusty-brown spore print, while jack-o’-lanterns can share the white spore print but have bright orange flesh and true orange gills.
records.caution_reason: Look-alike risk and GI intolerance risk; not a beginner shortcut mushroom.
records.flavor_texture_notes: Quality is best in young firm caps; stems can be fibrous.
records.quality_notes: Best young and firm; clustered caps can become slimy, buggy, or tough with age.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook completely; many foragers parboil or blanch first. Start with a small serving.
records.risk_reason: Honey mushrooms overlap with deadly Galerina, sulfur tuft, and jack-o’-lantern. Correctly identified honeys can still cause GI upset in some people.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Honey intolerance can cause GI upset; Galerina confusion can affect liver/kidneys; jack-o’-lantern and sulfur tuft confusion can cause GI poisoning.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Clustered growth on wood or buried roots, variable ring presence, tan/yellow-brown caps, and mixed wood-mushroom collections. White spore print is required for honey mushrooms.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat honey mushrooms without a white spore print and positive Armillaria/Desarmillaria identification. Any rusty-brown or purple-brown spore print means do not eat.

records.display_name: Deer mushroom group / Fawn shield (Pluteus cervinus group)
records.scientific_name: Pluteus cervinus group / deer mushroom complex
records.overview: Brown-capped wood mushroom with free gills that start pale and become pink as spores mature. This entry is anchored on the Pluteus cervinus / deer mushroom concept rather than a vague “brown mushroom” group: wood substrate, free pinking gills, pink-brown spore print, no ring, and no volva are the field pattern. Similar Pluteus species can require microscopy, so exact species naming should stay conservative.
records.notes: Brown-capped wood mushroom with free gills that start pale and become pink as spores mature. Included as a common gilled wood mushroom and pink-spored teaching entry.
records.general_notes: Brown-capped wood mushroom with free gills that start pale and become pink as spores mature. Included as a common gilled wood mushroom and pink-spored teaching entry.
records.field_identification: Look for a brown to gray-brown cap on dead wood or buried wood, free gills that do not attach to the stem, gills turning pink with age, a pinkish-brown spore print, no ring, no volva, and a whitish stem often marked with brownish fibrils. Similar Entoloma species have attached gills, not free gills.
records.culinary_uses: Edible in some field guides but not a beginner food here; quality is variable and the Pluteus cervinus concept includes cryptic relatives. Use as an ID-learning species unless expert-confirmed.
records.edibility_detail: Edible with caution but poor to fair quality, with an unremarkable flavor. Use only young, fresh, confidently identified specimens from wood, and do not rely on brown cap color alone.

edible_use.notes: Edible-with-caution / not a beginner mushroom. The pink spore print and free gills are useful ID clues, but species limits can be messy.

records.foraging_value: Low food value; mainly useful as an ID-learning mushroom
records.look_alike_notes: Deer mushrooms have free gills that turn pink and a brownish-pink spore print. Entoloma species can also become pink-spored, but their gills attach to the stem rather than staying free. Destroying angels and death caps have white spores plus Amanita base/ring features, and they grow from the ground rather than from dead wood. Dig up any pale-gilled mushroom base before ruling out Amanita.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring through fall.

records.overview: Small tan grassland mushroom famous for arcs or rings, but many unrelated mushrooms also form fairy rings. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution and cautionary lawn-mushroom entry.
records.notes: Small tan grassland mushroom famous for arcs or rings, but many unrelated mushrooms also form fairy rings. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution and cautionary lawn-mushroom entry.
records.general_notes: Small tan grassland mushroom famous for arcs or rings, but many unrelated mushrooms also form fairy rings. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution and cautionary lawn-mushroom entry.
records.field_identification: Look for small pale tan to buff caps in grass, well-spaced white to buff gills that attach to the stem but do not run down it, a white spore print, and a tough pliant stem that bends rather than snapping easily. Fairy-ring growth alone is not enough for identification.
records.culinary_uses: Edible in many guides, but this guide treats it as advanced because lawn mushrooms include dangerous look-alikes. Do not eat fairy-ring mushrooms without confirming every trait, especially white spore print, well-spaced gills, tough stem, and grassy habitat.
records.edibility_detail: Advanced edible-with-caution. The common name causes confusion because many mushrooms grow in rings.

edible_use.notes: Advanced edible-with-caution. The common name causes confusion because many mushrooms grow in rings.

records.foraging_value: Good edible for experienced identifiers; not a beginner lawn mushroom
records.look_alike_notes: Do not use the ring pattern alone: green-spored parasol, meadow mushrooms, and poisonous pale funnels can also appear in grass or rings. Fairy ring mushroom is smaller, tan, white-spored, has well-spaced gills, and a tough pliant stem; fool’s funnel has more crowded decurrent gills and muscarine risk.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in grass.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Marasmius oreades
use_links.notes: Grass habitat, well-spaced pale gills, white spore print, pale tan cap, and tough pliant stem.

records.notes: This is one of the few milkcaps where a species-level entry is practical: blue fruit body plus blue latex are field-distinctive, with only a few partial look-alikes.
records.field_identification: Latex: indigo blue to dark blue initially; blue latex and damaged tissue slowly stain greenish. Blue to gray-blue, often zoned; convex becoming depressed or funnel-shaped; can be sticky when young. Blue gills, attached to slightly decurrent, close; stain greenish when damaged or aging. Blue to gray-blue, central or slightly off-center; solid when young, sometimes hollowing with age; no ring or volva.

cap_color: Blue to gray-blue, often zoned; convex becoming depressed or funnel-shaped; can be sticky when young.
gill_color: Blue gills, attached to slightly decurrent, close; stain greenish when damaged or aging.
stem_color: Blue to gray-blue, central or slightly off-center; solid when young, sometimes hollowing with age; no ring or volva.
mushroom_profile.latex_abundance: variable; older/dry specimens may produce little latex
mushroom_profile.latex_color_change: blue latex and damaged tissue slowly stain greenish
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Blue latex is the central field character.
staining: Fresh blue surfaces fade gray-blue with age; cut or bruised tissue can shift greenish.
taste: Taste may be mild to slightly acrid. Do not promote acrid specimens as beginner food.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.scientific_name: North American Lactarius deliciosus-type group, including Lactarius deliciosus var. deterrimus sensu Kuo and Lactarius thyinos
records.notes: Group treatment is safer because the European name Lactarius deliciosus is commonly misapplied in North America, and eastern/northern taxa are still handled inconsistently in guides.
records.field_identification: Latex: orange initially; some taxa bruise green readily; Lactarius thyinos has orange milk and does not bruise green, with red staining around latex over time. Orange to salmon-orange, sometimes zoned; can develop green patches in greening taxa; convex becoming depressed. Orange to pale orange, attached to decurrent; green staining may appear in some taxa after handling. Orange to salmon, sometimes with shallow potholes/scrobicules; no ring or volva.

cap_color: Orange to salmon-orange, sometimes zoned; can develop green patches in greening taxa; convex becoming depressed.
gill_color: Orange to pale orange, attached to decurrent; green staining may appear in some taxa after handling.

smell: Mild, resinous, fruity, or not distinctive; odor should not be used alone.
taste: Usually mild to slightly bitter; reject hot/acrid specimens.

records.notes: Group treatment is safer because several white eastern milkcaps overlap heavily, and separating them often hinges on gill crowding, latex color change, cap texture, and taste.

staining: Some members slowly stain greenish, yellowish, or brown-spotted on gills; timing can take minutes and should be recorded.

records.scientific_name: Russula parvovirescens and related North American green-cracking Russulas formerly treated under Russula virescens concepts
records.notes: Edible green-cracking Russulas are practical, but the old R. virescens concept is taxonomically messy in North America. Group treatment avoids importing European certainty.
records.field_identification: Russula-style entry: brittle/chalky flesh and no latex when cut. Green, blue-green, or gray-green with cracked/quilted patches; often drier and patterned rather than smooth slimy green. White to cream; brittle; attached; no latex. White, brittle/chalky Russula stem; no ring or volva.

gill_color: White to cream; brittle; attached; no latex.
stem_color: White, brittle/chalky Russula stem; no ring or volva.
staining: Generally not a dramatic staining mushroom; cap may crack/quilt and fade with age.
taste: Only mild-tasting green-cracking Russulas belong here. Avoid acrid/hot Russulas.
host_trees: often oak/hardwood settings depending on member
host_filter_tokens: often oak/hardwood settings depending on member
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late June through fall in eastern North American/northern Great Lakes hardwood or mixed woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Amanita phalloides-like green Amanitas: Amanitas have ring/volva/free gills and fibrous flesh, not brittle Russula flesh deadly Amanita risk means base/ring/gill attachment must be checked Other green Russulas: may be smooth-capped rather than quilted/cracked; some require spore print/taste/chemistry/microscopy group entry should not overpromise species ID Acrid Russulas: hot/peppery taste, different cap patterns/colors avoid acrid Russulas
records.edibility_detail: Good edible group for advanced foragers when mild and confidently Russula, but keep app posture conservative. Require no ring, no volva, brittle flesh, no latex, and quilted/cracked green cap.

edible_use.notes: Good edible group for advanced foragers when mild and confidently Russula, but keep app posture conservative. Require no ring, no volva, brittle flesh, no latex, and quilted/cracked green cap.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
records.quality_notes: One of the better green Russula food candidates when mild and clearly quilted/cracked, but quality varies.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook confirmed mild specimens. Reject acrid, bitter, or non-Russula look-alikes.
records.risk_reason: Green Russula taxonomy is difficult, and greenish Amanitas are a deadly look-alike category if base/ring/flesh structure are not checked.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Acrid Russulas can cause GI upset; Amanita confusion can affect liver and kidneys and can be fatal.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Requires brittle Russula flesh, no latex, no ring, no volva, and quilted/cracked green cap. Smooth green caps and fibrous Amanita flesh are different problems.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat green Russulas unless brittle Russula structure, no latex, no ring, no volva, and mild taste are confirmed.

lookalike_risk.label: High — Russula/Amanita/acrid Russula confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Green Russulas need brittle Russula traits and no Amanita-style base/ring/volva. Acrid Russulas are a taste/toxicity caution.
required_checks: Confirm brittle, chalky Russula flesh and stem
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Any ring, volva, cup, bulb, latex, or hot/acrid taste means do not eat as green cracking Russula.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Acrid Russulas are not edible basket mushrooms.
Any Amanita-style base or ring means do not eat.

records.scientific_name: Russula emetica group and similar acrid red Russulas
records.notes: The practical app hazard is red Russulas with acrid/hot taste causing GI illness. Species-level precision is less useful than flagging the avoid group.
records.field_identification: Brittle/chalky Russula flesh; no latex; red to deep red cap; white to pale cream brittle gills; hot, acrid, or peppery taste. Red cap color alone is not enough for ID.

cap_color: Red to deep red, often darker centrally; smooth cap surface; cap skin may peel.
gill_color: White to pale cream, brittle Russula gills; no latex.
stem_color: White or pale, brittle/chalky; no ring or volva.
staining: Often not a strong bruising/staining entry; cap may fade or vary from bright to darker red.
smell: Usually not as useful as taste and habitat; some related red Russulas may have distinctive odors but this guide should not rely on smell alone.
taste: Hot, peppery, acrid, or burning taste is the warning trait. Do not recommend casual taste testing; if expert uses taste, spit out completely.
host_trees: spring through fall in Michigan per MSU source
host_filter_tokens: spring through fall in Michigan per MSU source
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to cream/pale yellow for many milkcaps and russulas; check species/group notes.

records.look_alike_notes: Shrimp Russula group: mild taste and fishy/shrimp odor with age; not hot/acrid do not eat red Russulas based on color Other mild red Russulas: mild taste, different odor/spore print/ecology; species ID often hard group-level caution is safer Amanita muscaria buttons or red/orange Amanitas: Amanitas have universal veil remnants, ring/volva/basal bulb features; Russulas are brittle and lack veil structures separate dangerous genus check
records.edibility_detail: Poisonous/avoid. Severe gastrointestinal upset is the app message; not deadly in typical North American treatment but absolutely not a food entry.

edible_use.notes: Poisonous/avoid. Severe gastrointestinal upset is the app message; not deadly in typical North American treatment but absolutely not a food entry.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
records.risk_reason: Acrid red Russulas can be confused with edible Russula groups, but hot/peppery taste is a stop sign and may predict gastrointestinal illness.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and colicky abdominal cramps, often within a few hours.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat red or purple Russulas that taste hot, peppery, or acrid; acrid Russulas belong in the sickener group and can cause vomiting and severe gastrointestinal upset.

records.food_quality: Common ID mushroom; not promoted as food
records.culinary_uses: Not promoted as food in this guide. Some sources discuss young mica caps, but this app treats them as an ID/caution group because inky caps and LBMs are easy to over-trust.
records.edibility_detail: Caution group. Do not use this record as a food recommendation; learn it mainly to separate clustered mica caps from alcohol inky caps and other small brown/gilled mushrooms.
records.overview: Common clustered inky-cap type on decaying or buried wood. Fresh caps can show tiny mica-like granules that rain may wash away.
records.notes: Look for honey-brown clustered caps, mica-like granules on young caps, grooved margins, gills that darken, and growth from wood, buried wood, or root systems.
records.general_notes: Group-scoped record to avoid brittle exact-species claims within the mica-cap complex.
records.field_identification: Dense clusters on decaying/buried wood; honey-brown caps with tiny glistening granules when fresh; gills pale then brown/black; black spore print. Granules may disappear after rain.

mushroom_profile.hard_rule: Do not treat clustered inky-cap/LBM mushrooms as beginner food.
